meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) mechanics items: kinematics, Newton's laws/forces, and gravitation. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by direct calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). Items requiring an un-capturable figure/graph, 5-option items, 'select two' items, and free-response items were skipped. Ch. 5 (Friction/Drag/Elasticity) test-prep page timed out on fetch and was skipped. One item (cpbs-xp034) is based on a figure whose forces are fully given in the question text.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements comparing position, distance traveled, and displacement is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An object may record a distance traveled of zero while recording a non-zero displacement.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero distance traveled while recording a displacement of zero.

choices.label: C
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero distance traveled while maintaining a position of zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An object may record a non-zero displacement while maintaining a position of zero.

questions.explanation: Distance is the total path length and is never negative; displacement is the net change in position. An object that travels out and returns to its start (a round trip) covers a non-zero distance but has zero displacement. The reverse (distance zero, displacement non-zero) is impossible.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 2 (Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the definitions of distance and displacement.

questions.stem: A student is trying to determine the acceleration of a feather as she drops it to the ground. If the student is looking to achieve a positive velocity and positive acceleration, what is the most sensible way to set up her coordinate system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her hand should be a coordinate of zero and the upward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Her hand should be a coordinate of zero and the downward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The floor should be a coordinate of zero and the upward direction should be considered positive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The floor should be a coordinate of zero and the downward direction should be considered positive.

questions.explanation: The feather moves and accelerates downward. To make both the velocity and the acceleration come out positive, the downward direction must be defined as positive. Placing the origin at the release point (her hand) also makes the position positive as the feather falls.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 2 (Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (sign of velocity/acceleration follows the chosen positive direction).

questions.stem: A ball is thrown at an angle of 45 degrees above the horizontal. Which of the following best describes the acceleration of the ball from the instant after it leaves the thrower's hand until the time it hits the ground?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always in the same direction as the motion, initially positive and gradually dropping to zero by the time it hits the ground

choices.label: B
choices.text: Initially positive in the upward direction, then zero at maximum height, then negative from there until it hits the ground

choices.label: C
choices.text: Always in the opposite direction as the motion, initially positive and gradually dropping to zero by the time it hits the ground

choices.label: D
choices.text: Always in the downward direction with the same constant value

questions.explanation: Once the ball is in free flight (neglecting air resistance), the only force on it is gravity, so its acceleration is constant: g directed downward at every instant, including at the top of the trajectory where the velocity is momentarily horizontal.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (projectile acceleration is constant g downward).

questions.stem: A student launches a ball with an initial horizontal velocity at an elevation 2 meters above ground. The ball follows a parabolic trajectory until it hits the ground. Which of the following accurately describes the graph of the ball's vertical acceleration versus time (taking the downward direction to be negative)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A negative value that does not change with time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gradually increasing negative value (straight line)

choices.label: C
choices.text: An increasing rate of negative values over time (parabolic curve)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zero at all times since the initial motion is horizontal

questions.explanation: During projectile motion the vertical acceleration is constant at -g (about -9.8 m/s² with downward negative). It does not change with time, so its graph is a horizontal line at a constant negative value. The initial horizontal launch does not change the vertical acceleration.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (vertical acceleration is the constant -g).

questions.stem: A ball is launched vertically upward. Which of the following correctly describes the graph of the ball's vertical velocity versus time, from just after launch until it returns to its starting height (taking up as positive)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Always positive, steadily decreasing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always positive, constant

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initially positive, steadily decreasing, becoming negative at the end

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initially zero, steadily getting more and more negative

questions.explanation: With constant downward acceleration (-g), the velocity decreases linearly. It starts positive (moving up), reaches zero at the highest point, then becomes negative as the ball falls back down. The graph is a straight line of constant negative slope crossing from positive to negative.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 3 (Two-Dimensional Kinematics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim; the source provided a position-vs-time data table, summarized here as 'launched vertically upward.' OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a body exerting a force on itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a person standing up from a seated position

choices.label: B
choices.text: a car accelerating while driving

choices.label: C
choices.text: both of the above

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of the above

questions.explanation: A body cannot produce a net force on itself; acceleration always requires an external force. A person standing up pushes against the floor (which pushes back), and a car accelerates because of friction between its tires and the road. In both cases the propelling force is external.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (acceleration requires a net external force).

questions.stem: What causes the force that moves a boat forward when someone rows it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The force is caused by the rower's arms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The force is caused by an interaction between the oars and gravity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force is caused by an interaction between the oars and the water the boat is traveling in.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is caused by friction.

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law, the oars push the water backward and the water pushes the oars (and the boat) forward. The forward thrust on the boat comes from this oar-water interaction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's third law.

questions.stem: A ball with a mass of 0.25 kg hits a gym ceiling with a force of 78.0 N. What is the net force on the ball at that instant? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2.50 N downward

choices.label: B
choices.text: 75.5 N downward

choices.label: C
choices.text: 78.0 N downward

choices.label: D
choices.text: 80.5 N downward

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law the ceiling pushes back on the ball with 78.0 N downward. The ball's weight, mg = 0.25 × 9.8 = 2.45 N, also acts downward. The net force is 78.0 + 2.45 ≈ 80.5 N downward.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by combining the reaction force and the weight.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Earth exerts a force due to gravity on your body, and your body exerts a smaller force on the Earth, because your mass is smaller than the mass of the Earth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Moon orbits the Earth because the Earth exerts a force on the Moon and the Moon exerts a force equal in magnitude and direction on the Earth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A rocket taking off exerts a force on the Earth equal to the force the Earth exerts on the rocket.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An airplane cruising at a constant speed is not affected by gravity.

questions.explanation: Newton's third law: interacting bodies exert forces equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, regardless of their masses. So the rocket and Earth exert equal-magnitude forces on each other. Option A is wrong (the forces are equal, not smaller), B is wrong (the forces are opposite in direction), and D is wrong (gravity always acts).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's third law.

questions.stem: A force is applied to accelerate an object on a smooth icy surface. When the force stops, which of the following will be true? (Assume zero friction.)

choices.label: A
choices.text: The object's acceleration becomes zero.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The object's speed becomes zero.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The object's acceleration continues to increase at a constant rate.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The object accelerates, but in the opposite direction.

questions.explanation: With no applied force and no friction, the net force is zero, so by Newton's first/second law the acceleration is zero. The object keeps moving at constant velocity (its speed does not drop to zero).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from Newton's first law.

questions.stem: Two water-skiers, with masses of 48 kg and 61 kg, are towed behind the same boat. When the boat accelerates, the rope the skiers hold onto exerts a net force of 290 N on the skiers. At what rate will the skiers accelerate?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2.7 m/s²

choices.label: C
choices.text: 6.0 m/s² and 4.8 m/s²

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5.3 m/s²

questions.explanation: The skiers are towed together by the single rope, so treat them as one system of mass 48 + 61 = 109 kg. a = F/m = 290 N / 109 kg ≈ 2.7 m/s². They share the same acceleration, so a single value (not two) is correct.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (a = F/total mass).

questions.stem: An archer shoots an arrow straight up with a force of 24.5 N. The arrow has a mass of 0.4 kg. What is the force of gravity on the arrow? (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 9.8 m/s²

questions.explanation: The force of gravity (weight) is W = mg = 0.4 kg × 9.8 m/s² = 3.92 ≈ 3.9 N. The 24.5 N launch force is a distractor, and option A has the wrong units (it is an acceleration, not a force).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (W = mg).

questions.stem: Two teams are engaging in a tug-of-war. The rope suddenly snaps. Which statement is true about the forces involved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The forces exerted by the two teams are no longer equal; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The forces exerted by the players are no longer balanced by the force of tension in the rope; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force of gravity balances the forces exerted by the players; the teams will fall as a result.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force of tension in the rope is transferred to the players; the teams will accelerate in opposite directions as a result.

questions.explanation: While the rope is taut, each team's pull (transmitted through their feet pushing the ground) is balanced by the rope tension, so they stay in place. When the rope snaps, the tension vanishes and each team's force is no longer balanced, so each team accelerates backward—in opposite directions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (loss of tension unbalances each team's force).

questions.stem: Which statement is true about drawing free-body diagrams?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram should be the last step in solving a problem about forces.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram helps you compare forces quantitatively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The forces in a free-body diagram should always balance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drawing a free-body diagram can help you determine the net force.

questions.explanation: A free-body diagram shows all forces acting on a single object, which lets you add them to find the net force. It is an early step (not the last), the forces only balance in equilibrium (not always), and it primarily organizes forces rather than comparing them quantitatively.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the purpose of a free-body diagram.

questions.stem: A block slides on a horizontal surface. The forces on it are a gravitational force of 40 N (down), a normal force of 40 N (up), and a frictional force directed to the left. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is 0.20. The acceleration of the block is most nearly: (Take g = 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1.0 m/s² to the right

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1.0 m/s² to the left

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.0 m/s² to the right

choices.label: D
choices.text: 2.0 m/s² to the left

questions.explanation: The block's mass is m = W/g = 40/9.8 ≈ 4.08 kg. The only horizontal force is kinetic friction, f = μN = 0.20 × 40 = 8.0 N, directed left (opposing the rightward sliding). So a = f/m = 8.0/4.08 ≈ 2.0 m/s², directed to the left.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Based on OpenStax Figure 4.48; all forces are fully specified in the question text (no image needed). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

questions.stem: Which phenomenon correctly describes the direction and magnitude of normal forces?

choices.label: A
choices.text: electromagnetic attraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: electromagnetic repulsion

choices.label: C
choices.text: gravitational attraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravitational repulsion

questions.explanation: The normal force arises from the electromagnetic repulsion between the electron clouds of atoms in the two surfaces in contact. This repulsion acts perpendicular to the surface and prevents the objects from interpenetrating.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (normal force is electromagnetic repulsion between atoms).

questions.stem: Which of the basic forces best explains tension in a rope being pulled between two people? Is the acting force causing attraction or repulsion in this instance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: gravity; attraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: electromagnetic; attraction

choices.label: C
choices.text: weak and strong nuclear; attraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: weak and strong nuclear; repulsion

questions.explanation: Tension is transmitted by the electromagnetic forces (interatomic bonds) holding the rope's molecules together. Because the rope resists being pulled apart, the relevant interatomic forces are attractive.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 4 (Dynamics) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (tension is electromagnetic and attractive at the atomic level).

questions.stem: Jupiter has a mass approximately 300 times greater than Earth's and a radius about 11 times greater. How will the gravitational acceleration at the surface of Jupiter compare to that at the surface of the Earth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Greater

choices.label: B
choices.text: Less

choices.label: C
choices.text: About the same

choices.label: D
choices.text: Not enough information

questions.explanation: Surface gravity is g = GM/R². Relative to Earth, Jupiter's value scales as 300/(11²) = 300/121 ≈ 2.5. Since the factor is greater than 1, Jupiter's surface gravity is greater than Earth's.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation (g ∝ M/R²).

questions.stem: In the formula g = GM/r², what does G represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The acceleration due to gravity

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gravitational constant that is the same everywhere in the universe

choices.label: C
choices.text: A gravitational constant that is inversely proportional to the radius

choices.label: D
choices.text: The factor by which you multiply the inertial mass to obtain the gravitational mass

questions.explanation: G is the universal gravitational constant (about 6.67 × 10^-11 N·m²/kg²). It has the same value everywhere; the symbol g (lowercase) is the local acceleration due to gravity, which does depend on M and r.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (G is the universal gravitational constant).

questions.stem: A recently discovered planet has a mass twice as great as Earth's and a radius twice as large as Earth's. What will be the approximate size of its gravitational field at the surface? (Earth's surface gravity is 9.8 m/s².)

choices.label: A
choices.text: 19 m/s²

choices.label: B
choices.text: 4.9 m/s²

choices.label: C
choices.text: 2.5 m/s²

choices.label: D
choices.text: 9.8 m/s²

questions.explanation: g = GM/R², so relative to Earth the field scales as (2)/(2²) = 2/4 = 0.5. Thus g ≈ 0.5 × 9.8 = 4.9 m/s².
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 6 (Gravitation and Uniform Circular Motion) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
